graph_vector_fulltext.message:
From the provided text, the transparency “chain” for AI‑generated/manipulated content is described at a high level, but it does not spell out a full step-by-step list (or deep-fake-specific workflow) in the excerpts you shared.

What the AI system provider must do (transparency/supporting obligations)
- Provide “transparency, including the accompanying instructions for use,” to assist deployers in using the system and making informed decisions.  
- In those instructions for use, include information relevant to risks (including “risks to health and safety” and “risks to fundamental rights”), and make them available to “target deployers” in “easily understood language.”  
- Identify relevant human oversight measures (and the instructions for use should reflect the applicable “human oversight arrangements”).  
- For high-risk AI systems specifically, the provider carries out conformity assessment and must register the high-risk AI system in the EU database (and ensure “substantial modification” is registered as well).

What the deployer must do when creating deep fakes (transparency obligations)
- The excerpts state that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content” can raise deception/impersonation risks and therefore are subject to “specific transparency obligations.”  
- Those transparency obligations are described as relating to “detection and disclosure of artificially generated or manipulated outputs” and “labelling of artificially generated content.”  
- Separately, deployers have a “notification obligation” that “concerns interaction with” an AI system, and natural persons “should be notified” of interaction with/exposure to an AI system (this is phrased generally in the provided text, not limited to deep fakes).

How this interacts with the Digital Services Act (VLOPs/VLOSEs)
- The text explicitly links the AI transparency obligations on AI-generated/manipulated outputs with the DSA framework: the “detection and disclosure” obligations “facilitate implementation of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 (Digital Services Act).”  
- Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 is noted as imposing obligations on “providers of very large online platforms” and “providers of very large online search engines,” and as establishing a “risk-management framework.”  
- The excerpt also mentions DSA “provisions regarding the liability of providers of intermediary services” and DSA obligations such as an “obligation to process notices on illegal content,” but it does not detail how deep-fake labelling/disclosure should be operationalized by VLOPs/VLOSEs beyond the general connection above.

If you want a “complete” chain (provider → deployer → VLOP/VLOSE) with concrete required actions (e.g., exactly what label must say, where it must appear, exceptions, and which actor bears which duty in which scenario), I’d need the specific AI Act article(s)/recitals that set the deep-fake transparency duty and the specific DSA provisions you want mapped—those details are not present in the provided excerpts.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: Trace the complete transparency obligation chain for AI-generated content: What must the AI system provider do, what must the deployer do when creating deep fakes, and how does this interact with obligations under the Digital Services Act for very large online platforms?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
From the provided text, the transparency “chain” for AI‑generated/manipulated content is described at a high level, but it does not spell out a full step-by-step list (or deep-fake-specific workflow) in the excerpts you shared.

What the AI system provider must do (transparency/supporting obligations)
- Provide “transparency, including the accompanying instructions for use,” to assist deployers in using the system and making informed decisions.  
- In those instructions for use, include information relevant to risks (including “risks to health and safety” and “risks to fundamental rights”), and make them available to “target deployers” in “easily understood language.”  
- Identify relevant human oversight measures (and the instructions for use should reflect the applicable “human oversight arrangements”).  
- For high-risk AI systems specifically, the provider carries out conformity assessment and must register the high-risk AI system in the EU database (and ensure “substantial modification” is registered as well).

What the deployer must do when creating deep fakes (transparency obligations)
- The excerpts state that “certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content” can raise deception/impersonation risks and therefore are subject to “specific transparency obligations.”  
- Those transparency obligations are described as relating to “detection and disclosure of artificially generated or manipulated outputs” and “labelling of artificially generated content.”  
- Separately, deployers have a “notification obligation” that “concerns interaction with” an AI system, and natural persons “should be notified” of interaction with/exposure to an AI system (this is phrased generally in the provided text, not limited to deep fakes).

How this interacts with the Digital Services Act (VLOPs/VLOSEs)
- The text explicitly links the AI transparency obligations on AI-generated/manipulated outputs with the DSA framework: the “detection and disclosure” obligations “facilitate implementation of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 (Digital Services Act).”  
- Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 is noted as imposing obligations on “providers of very large online platforms” and “providers of very large online search engines,” and as establishing a “risk-management framework.”  
- The excerpt also mentions DSA “provisions regarding the liability of providers of intermediary services” and DSA obligations such as an “obligation to process notices on illegal content,” but it does not detail how deep-fake labelling/disclosure should be operationalized by VLOPs/VLOSEs beyond the general connection above.

If you want a “complete” chain (provider → deployer → VLOP/VLOSE) with concrete required actions (e.g., exactly what label must say, where it must appear, exceptions, and which actor bears which duty in which scenario), I’d need the specific AI Act article(s)/recitals that set the deep-fake transparency duty and the specific DSA provisions you want mapped—those details are not present in the provided excerpts.

Content: Text Content:
 by the public, the EU database, including  the information made available through it, should comply with requirements under the Directive (EU) 2019/882. (132) Certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of  impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not. In certain circumstances, the use  of these systems should therefore be subject to specific transparency obligations without prejudice to the  requirements and obligations for high-risk AI
----
 certain situations those  operators could act in more than one role at the same time and should therefore fulfil cumulatively all relevant  obligations associated with those roles. For example, an operator could act as a distributor and an importer at the  same time. (84) To ensure legal certainty, it is necessary to clarify that, under certain specific conditions, any distributor, importer,  deployer or other third-party should be considered to be a provider of a high-risk AI system
----
) Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 19 October 2022 on a Single Market For Digital  Services and amending Directive 2000/31/EC (Digital Services Act) (OJ L 277, 27.10.2022, p. 1).
----
, under which the AI system  can lead to risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights, on the changes that have been pre-determined and  assessed for conformity by the provider and on the relevant human oversight measures, including the measures to  facilitate the interpretation of the outputs of the AI system by the deployers. Transparency, including the  accompanying instructions for use, should assist deployers in the use of the system and support informed decision  making by them. Deployers should, inter

chunks.text: certain situations those operators could act in more than one role at the same time and should therefore fulfil cumulatively all relevant obligations associated with those roles. For example, an operator could act as a distributor and an importer at the same time. (84) To ensure legal certainty, it is necessary to clarify that, under certain specific conditions, any distributor, importer, deployer or other third-party should be considered to be a provider of a high-risk AI system

by the public, the EU database, including the information made available through it, should comply with requirements under the Directive (EU) 2019/882. (132) Certain AI systems intended to interact with natural persons or to generate content may pose specific risks of impersonation or deception irrespective of whether they qualify as high-risk or not. In certain circumstances, the use of these systems should therefore be subject to specific transparency obligations without prejudice to the requirements and obligations for high-risk AI

) Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 19 October 2022 on a Single Market For Digital Services and amending Directive 2000/31/EC (Digital Services Act) (OJ L 277, 27.10.2022, p. 1).

, under which the AI system can lead to risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights, on the changes that have been pre-determined and assessed for conformity by the provider and on the relevant human oversight measures, including the measures to facilitate the interpretation of the outputs of the AI system by the deployers. Transparency, including the accompanying instructions for use, should assist deployers in the use of the system and support informed decision making by them. Deployers should, inter
